text: welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery today we're going to be talking about elements of an operation i'm going to be focusing on etep ventral hernia and the critical steps of planning how you dock and crossover the case we're presenting is a sixty-two-year-old man who presented with morbid obesity and medication-induced lipodystrophy he had a recurrent and incarcerated ventral hernia I had worked with this patient for many, many months to try and optimize weight, but unfortunately, because of other factors, BMI could not be reduced below forty-four. And I felt that a minimally invasive approach to hernia repair would be in the best interest of the patient for both short-term and long-term recovery potential. So what is ETAP? It's Enhanced View Totally Extraperitoneal Access, much like to have access for amoral hernias, ETEP takes advantage of being able to perform a complex operation outside of the visceral cavity. And for abdominal wall reconstruction ventral hernia repair, we're working in the retromuscular space, anteriorly finding the rectus muscle, posteriorly the rectus sheath. ETEP has been shown to allow us to do extraperitoneal placement of mesh, develop a unified plane spanning the entire abdominal wall, and can help minimize the need for circumferential fixation. It really has taken off over the course of the last five to ten years.

Whenever planning any abdominal operation, a ventral hernia repair, it's really important to review your cross-sectional imaging, evaluating defect size, hernia contents, evidence of prior mesh, evidence of any metallic fixation, and then specifically for patients who are undergoing a possible ETEP approach looking at your rectus with your integrity of the abdominal wall, and then understanding what your potential component separation is. So this patient had very favorable anatomy, very wide rectus, over a twelve to fourteen centimeters, had a untouched upper abdomen, and then had a peri-umbilical EHSM-III hernia with a five centimeter width on the neck and then about a three to four centimeter width a large volume of incarcerated omentum and a small loop of bowel involved in this hernia, I felt that they would be a great candidate for ETEP. And so for a lot of my trainings, whenever they're preparing for abdominal wall surgery, I really recommend setting yourself up for success by doing what I call abdominal wall mapping. Looking at your axial and sagittal views on your ct can help you review the abdominal wall understand if they have what the hernia is understand what the diastasis is if there is one and then really plan out how you're going to approach this the size of the mess you may need and where you're going to dock

The surgeon sets up for the E-TEP approach using basic laparoscopic equipment for initial access. Robotic instruments include a force bipolar, monopolar scissors, and a mega suture cut needle driver. The patient's arms are tucked, and the initial retromuscular access is achieved using a KII first entry trocar. The surgeon targets a central rectus initial port, visualizes the rectus muscle, and flattens their hands to facilitate entry through the subcutaneous anterior sheath.

The surgeon's hands are parallel to the floor as insufflation is initiated. The posterior sheath is observed stuck to the trocar tip, which is then pulled back to enter the areolar plane between the rectus muscle and posterior sheath. The surgeon performs retro-rectus dissection, focusing on medial and lateral dissection towards the semilunar line, aiming to place three robotic trocars medial to this line for strategic port placement.

The surgeon places the first robotic trocar medial to the semilunar line and begins developing the space using basic laparoscopic equipment. Electrocautery is employed with a Maryland dissector or Hunter grasper to maintain a bloodless field and efficiently dissect the initial entry site, expanding the space for the ETEP approach.

The surgeon bluntly dissects areolar fibers around the hernia site, using energy devices to control potential bleeding. Visible atypical scarring patterns are present in the retromuscular space due to prior surgeries. The surgeon places the second trocar lower and transitions the camera to a newly placed trocar, developing space for a third robotic port. The initial entry trocar remains centrally placed in the rectus muscle, not used for robotic operations.

The surgeon identifies transversus abdominis fibers through the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique, indicating the initiation point for a top-down TAR approach. A trocar is placed in the upper abdomen, and robotic docking is performed. The crossover technique is initiated by incising medially on the posterior sheath, preserving the linea alba, and entering the preperitoneal space. The surgeon works towards the contralateral rectus sheath, incising to enter the contralateral retro-rectus space, with the lateral margin being the contralateral semilunar line.

The surgeon initiates crossover by incising the posterior sheath and releasing it to enter the preperitoneal fat pad. The dissection reveals a narrow linea alba, approximately two centimeters wide, without diastasis in the upper abdomen. The surgeon performs retromuscular dissection, encountering a medial perforator, and works to achieve hemostasis while continuing the dissection down the abdominal wall towards the hernia.

So there we are, an ETEP docking and crossover. I tried to do minimal editing here to just give a full sense of how this is done and for trainees how to prepare for this operation to really make that first part very educational. So ETEP ventral hernia, obviously special pre-operative planning important to have a thorough understanding of the dogma wall anatomy and then patient selection is really critical especially early in the learning curve thank you very much and i hope you enjoyed this presentation
